response:
The differential equation provided is a nonlinear ordinary differential equation with a time-dependent coefficient:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = 0.3P \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)P
\]
Simplifying the equation:
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = 0.3P - \frac{0.3}{1000}P^2 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)P
\]
\[
\frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) - \frac{0.3}{1000}P \right]
\]
This equation does not possess a closed-form general solution in terms of elementary functions because the coefficient of the linear term varies sinusoidally with time. Standard logistic equations with constant parameters can be solved using separation of variables or Bernoulli substitution, but the introduction of the time-dependent term \( A \sin(\omega t) \) prevents exact analytical integration.

To address the request for a general solution, one must rely on numerical methods (such as Runge-Kutta) or perturbation theory (assuming \( A \) is small) to approximate the behavior. If we treat the sinusoidal term as a small perturbation, the population \( P(t) \) will oscillate around the carrying capacity \( K \) with a phase shift determined by the interaction between the intrinsic growth rate \( r \) and the seasonal forcing.

Regarding the second part of the inquiry, the peak population occurs when the growth rate \( \frac{dP}{dt} \) transitions from positive to negative. The sinusoidal term \( 0.1 \sin(\frac{\pi t}{6}) \) represents the seasonal rainfall influence. The sine function reaches its maximum value of 1 when the argument \( \frac{\pi t}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2} \), which corresponds to \( t = 3 \) months (March). This indicates the strongest positive growth boost from rainfall occurs in spring.
